svg.aria-label: Animated diagram: an insert appends a live value record; an update appends a new record and flags the old one Updated; a delete flags the record Tombstone. When garbage passes the waste threshold, GC rewrites the surviving records into a new file, writes a journal and commit marker, then swaps the file inside a swap epoch and re-points each key by compare-and-set.
svg.text: Value log: writes, deletes, and garbage collection
Nothing is ever overwritten in place. Updates and deletes only flag records — GC is what actually reclaims the bytes.

g.text: put(k1, v1): the value is appended as a live record, then the LSM maps k1 → (page 0, segment 1). Both happen under the bucket write lock.
put(k2, v2): another append. The value log only ever grows at the head — no in-place rewrites.
put(k1, v1′) — an update appends a NEW record and flags the old one Updated (0x02). k1 is re-pointed; the old bytes are now garbage.
delete(k2) flags its record Tombstone (0x01) and writes an LSM tombstone. The bytes stay on disk until GC.
Garbage accumulates: 2 of 4 records are dead. The bucket's waste ratio (50%) is now past the 30% GC threshold.
GC takes the bucket write lock — the same lock put/delete hold — then scans the LSM for this bucket's live pointers.
Survivors are rewritten into a fresh page in value_log_3.log.new — they get NEW segment ids. Pages below the waste threshold are copied byte-for-byte instead.
Before the swap: the GC journal (key → new pointer) and its commit marker are fsynced, so a crash mid-swap is recoverable either way.
Swap epoch: the bucket generation goes ODD, the file is renamed in, and each key is re-pointed by compare-and-set under its existing sequence.
Generation is EVEN again and the dead bytes are gone. The journal is deleted only after the memtable flush makes the new pointers durable.

svg.text: RECORDS IN THE BUCKET FILE (LIVE vs GARBAGE)
Odd generation = swap in progress; readers retry. See the read diagram.
